figures.caption: Table 7 Comparison of model complexity across different gaze estimation methods. "Hybrid" indicates models that combine CNN and MoE transformer and CLIP, while "Trans." refers to Transformer-based models. Parameters are reported in millions (M) and FLOPs in giga (G).
Figure 1: Gaze visualization of the proposed GMGaze on unseen frames from public videos.

Figure 4: Overall training paradigm of cross-domain setting. During training, a domain discriminator is jointly optimized with the GMGaze to perform adversarial domain adaptation, reducing the distribution discrepancy between source and target domains. Meanwhile, the absolute cosine similarity between the two global semantic feature vectors (𝒇 1 and 𝒇 2) is minimized to separate them into orthogonal directions in the feature space, encouraging the model to learn more gaze-related and domain-invariant representations. Finally, the model yields the 3D gaze direction prediction (𝑑𝑥, 𝑑𝑦, 𝑑𝑧).
Figure 6: Visualization of predictions in within dataset (1-2 row) and cross-domain (3-4 row) evaluation. Green arrows represents the ground truth, red arrows means the predictions.

Figure 2: Flowchart of GMGaze. Left: Pre-defined prompt banks (Illuminations, Backgrounds, Head poses, and Descriptions) are encoded by the frozen CLIP text encoder to initialize learnable semantic prototype banks. Middle: A CLIP image encoder outputs a global embedding and patch tokens; a CNN provides high-resolution local tokens. The semantic tokens T𝐼1, T𝐻1, T𝐵1, and T𝑑 encode key contextual dimensions: Illumination, Head pose, Background, and Description. Prototype similarity and discrete selection produce two conditioned global tokens 𝑓1 and 𝑓2, which are assembled with patch (𝑇patch) and CNN (𝑇cnn) tokens. Right: All tokens are projected to a unified dimension, concatenated, and processed by stacked transformer layers whose Feed-Forward Networks (FFN) are replaced by routed-and-shared MoE experts to predict the 3D gaze direction (𝑑𝑥, 𝑑𝑦, 𝑑𝑧).
Figure 9: Example of failure cases from cross-domain setting using the EYEDIAP as target domain.

Figure 7: Comparison of MoE expert load across datasets (within-domain setting). Top: selection proportion (percentage of token assignments per expert). Bottom: normalized weight proportion (sum of gating weights per expert, normalized).
Figure 8: Ablation study of learning rate’s effect on mean angular error across datasets M, E, G, and Et.

Figure 5: Example for samples from the four benchmarks (EYEDIAP, Gaze360, ETH-XGaze, MPIIFaceGaze), showing diversity in illumination, head pose, background, and appearance (First row). 2D gaze direction distributions (yaw vs. pitch, degrees) for these datasets (Second row). Each point corresponds to a labeled gaze sample; the colormap encodes log-scaled sample density (warmer colors = higher frequency).

Table 3 Comparison of gaze estimation (GE) error in degrees under crossdomain evaluation. Lower values indicate better performance. The best result is highlighted in bold. DG denotes domain generalization (no target-domain samples used), and FT denotes the number of target-domain samples used for fine-tuning. Part of the results are taken from [14].
Table 2 Comparison of GE error (in degrees) on multiple datasets.
Table 1 The predefined text prompt vocabulary used for prototype initialization.

Figure 3: (Left): Example of dense transformer layer, which consists of multi head attention (MHA) followed by a FFN. (Right): MoE transformer layer in which a FFN block is replaced by a set of experts, which operate in parallel. The final prediction head ultimately yields the 3D gaze direction predictions (𝑑𝑥, 𝑑𝑦, 𝑑𝑧).
